evals.prompt: Our GitHub Actions CI takes 45 minutes. We have 400 Playwright tests, 2000 Jest unit tests, ESLint, TypeScript. Where do we start?
evals.expected_output: Should check qa-context.md or ask. Should walk through profiling first: identify the long pole (almost certainly Playwright at this scale), then shard it with matrix workers (`playwright test --shard=${{ matrix.shard }}/N` with `strategy.matrix.shard: [1,2,3,4]`). Run Jest in parallel with --shard or pytest-xdist-style. Move full E2E to post-merge if PR-time is critical. Add `concurrency` group with `cancel-in-progress: true` to kill obsolete runs. Cache `~/.npm` / Playwright browsers / Docker layers. Should warn about test isolation requirements for sharding. Should mention measuring savings step by step. Cross-references playwright and jest-vitest.
assertions: Reads qa-context.md or asks for it
Profiles first to identify the long pole
Mentions Jest sharding for unit tests

evals.prompt: Our tests pass locally but our CI matrix flakes when running 8 parallel shards. Same tests, same code.
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose shared mutable state across parallel jobs: (1) all shards talk to the same Postgres instance / Redis / queue — DB rows collide; (2) shards write to the same S3 bucket / same file paths; (3) unique constraints hit by parallel inserts of identically-named test users; (4) port conflicts; (5) shared cache layer at SUT side. Recommend: namespace test data per shard (`worker_id` / `CI_NODE_INDEX`-prefixed IDs), use per-shard schemas or databases, isolate S3 prefixes per shard. Cross-reference test-data-management and flaky-test-management. Should NOT recommend reducing parallelism as the fix.
assertions: Recommends per-shard DB schemas / S3 prefixes
Refuses to recommend reducing parallelism as fix

evals.prompt: how do i aggregate junit xml from sharded jobs
evals.expected_output: Should treat the casual phrasing as a valid trigger. Should outline the pattern: each sharded job uploads its JUnit XML as an artifact named uniquely (`results-shard-1.xml`, etc.); a follow-up job (`needs: [test]`, `if: always()`) downloads all artifacts and runs an aggregator. Options: language-specific aggregators (`junit-merge`), or push to a service (Datadog CI Visibility, Buildkite Test Analytics, Trunk, Allure server). Should show a minimal GitHub Actions snippet using `actions/upload-artifact` + `actions/download-artifact`. Should mention `if: always()` is critical so reports get aggregated even when tests fail. Cross-references test-reporting.

evals.prompt: Should we add `--retries 3` to our PR CI to deal with flake?
evals.expected_output: Should clearly say no — high-retry policies hide systemic issues. Recommend instead: (1) baseline the flake rate; (2) identify top-N flakiest tests; (3) for each, root-cause and fix (most flake comes from a small subset of tests); (4) if retries are necessary as a stopgap, retry once max AND mark retried tests as flaky in the report (not silent pass); (5) track flake rate over time. Should cross-reference flaky-test-management. Should NOT pretend retries solve flake — they just delay the cost.
assertions: Allows retry-once with reporting as a stopgap
Does not pretend retries solve flake

evals.prompt: Our CI cost is $4000/month. Can we cut it without losing coverage?
evals.expected_output: Should give cost-discipline levers: (1) `concurrency` with `cancel-in-progress: true` — kills obsolete runs from rapid pushes; (2) right-size runners (4 vCPU may be enough when you're paying for 16); (3) spot / preemptible runners for non-critical paths; (4) move heavy checks (full E2E, perf, mutation) to nightly scheduled runs instead of every PR; (5) test impact analysis on PR runs to skip unrelated suites; (6) aggressive caching; (7) right-size matrix (drop redundant cells); (8) self-hosted runners if volume justifies. Should warn about the false economy of cutting too much — broken signal is worse than $4000. Cross-reference test-strategy.

evals.prompt: Test impact analysis sounds great. Should we adopt it now? Our suite has 30% flake rate.
evals.expected_output: Should advise no — TIA assumes the suite gives reliable signal; 30% flake rate means the signal is broken. Recommend: (1) fix flake first (cross-reference flaky-test-management) — TIA on a flaky suite picks the wrong tests to run and the wrong tests to skip; (2) get baseline flake under 1-2% before considering TIA; (3) when ready, start with simple file-graph TIA (Jest --changedSince, vitest related) before adopting commercial TIA tools (Launchable, etc.). Should warn that TIA is a force multiplier — it amplifies whatever your suite quality is. Cross-references flaky-test-management and test-strategy.
assertions: Advises against TIA with 30% flake rate
Recommends starting with simple file-graph TIA
Frames TIA as a force multiplier
